title: Quick check: process detections and benign-but-suspicious activity

questions.prompt: Why do legitimate IT and attackers use overlapping toolkits (PowerShell, PsExec, scheduled tasks)?
choices: The toolkits are useful for the same reasons, deep OS reach, remote execution, persistence, so both legitimate admins and attackers gravitate to them.

choices.label: Attackers deliberately copy IT toolkits to confuse defenders.
choices.rationale: Partial framing; the lesson's point is structural (the tools are inherently useful), not deceptive.

choices.label: Windows only ships with a small set of administrative tools.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing. The toolkits exist because they're powerful, not because the OS is constrained.

choices.label: RMMs and attackers both use the same vendors' command-and-control infrastructure.
choices.rationale: Fabricated overlap.

questions.explanation: The OS doesn't know which is which; that's why the SOC adds context.

questions.prompt: How does the SOC mark a benign-but-suspicious detection differently from a malicious one?
choices: Lower severity (Low or High, not Critical); a lighter Recommendation; Evidence that names the tool AND the context (e.g. "PsExec from RMM-SERVER-01 per the customer's documented RMM pattern").

choices.label: The SOC doesn't raise benign-but-suspicious detections, only malicious ones.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The SOC raises both; the tech reads the Recommendation to know which is which.

choices.label: Benign-but-suspicious detections have no Recommendation field.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Every Incident Report has a Recommendation.

choices.label: Benign-but-suspicious detections are sent only to the senior, not the helpdesk tech.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the routing model.

questions.explanation: The tech reads the Recommendation. If 'no action required, legitimate', no action is required.

questions.prompt: Failure mode A: "Oh, that's just our RMM." What's the failure?
choices: The tech dismisses an incident as routine RMM activity without reading the Recommendation, but sometimes the SOC raised the incident because the RMM-shaped activity was actually attacker activity disguising itself as the RMM.

choices.label: The tech dismisses an incident even after the Recommendation says to act.
choices.rationale: Cruder form. The lesson's specific framing is dismissal-without-reading.

choices.label: The tech assumes the RMM is always legitimate even when there's no incident.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing. The failure is specifically inside the incident-triage workflow.

choices.label: The tech investigates the RMM source instead of acting on the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Investigation isn't the named failure here; dismissal is.

questions.explanation: The dismissal-without-reading is what makes it dangerous. Read the Recommendation first.

questions.prompt: Failure mode B: "PowerShell encoded command, must be malicious." What's the failure?
choices: The tech reaches for the Critical reflex based on the OS-level pattern without reading the Recommendation, but encoded PowerShell can be a legitimate admin script the customer's IT runs nightly, classified by the SOC as legitimate-but-flagged.

choices.label: The tech escalates every encoded PowerShell detection to a senior even after the senior says not to.
choices.rationale: That's a coarser failure; the lesson's specific framing is pattern-as-substitute-for-Recommendation.

choices.label: The tech never reads encoded PowerShell commands.
choices.rationale: Not the failure. The failure is acting on pattern alone.

choices.label: The tech files an exclusion for all PowerShell encoded commands.
choices.rationale: That's a 5.6-style failure (broad exclusion). The 5.5 framing is escalation-by-pattern.

questions.explanation: Escalation-by-pattern misses the SOC's actual classification.

questions.prompt: The lesson's two-question test for process detections. What are the two questions?
choices: (1) Did the analyst say to do this in the Recommendation? (2) Is there new signal the analyst didn't have?

choices.label: (1) Is the source the RMM? (2) Is the command encoded?
choices.rationale: Pattern-based questions, not Recommendation-based. The whole point is the test isn't pattern-based.

choices.label: (1) Is the severity Low? (2) Is the host a workstation?
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's test.

choices.label: (1) Has the customer's IT confirmed it? (2) Is the user available to ask?
choices.rationale: Customer confirmation can be new signal but isn't the test itself.

questions.explanation: The test is Recommendation plus new signal, not pattern matching.

questions.prompt: Low EDR incident, WS-EXAMPLE-IT-02 (customer's IT staff workstation). Title: "Suspicious PsExec usage detected." Evidence: PsExec from WS-EXAMPLE-IT-02 to WS-EXAMPLE-RECEPTION-01. Recommendation: "No action required. Activity matches the customer's documented internal IT process. Closing as legitimate." What's the right action?
choices: Close the incident with no action, per the Recommendation.

choices.label: Investigate PsExec usage to confirm it's really the customer's IT process.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex. The investigation happened; re-investigating from the tech seat is second-guessing the SOC.

choices.label: Approve a remediation 'to be safe' even though none is recommended.
choices.rationale: Worst response. No remediation in the Recommendation; acting outside it.

choices.label: File an exclusion for PsExec on WS-EXAMPLE-IT-02 so it doesn't fire again.
choices.rationale: Wrong timing and wrong shape. Exclusions need multiple SOC-classified-benign incidents first.

questions.explanation: The SOC classified it; the Recommendation is in the action zone; 'no action required' IS the action.

questions.prompt: Low EDR incident on WS-EXAMPLE-MARKETING-04. Title: "PowerShell encoded command." Evidence: encoded payload reaches a known-bad domain. Recommendation: "Approve the autoruns remediation. This is not legitimate admin activity." What's the right action?
choices: Run the standard EDR workflow per the Recommendation: claim, review, approve the remediation, verify, close, document.

choices.label: Treat as Critical because 'PowerShell encoded command always means malicious'.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex. Escalation-by-pattern; the SOC tagged it Low with a specific remediation.

choices.label: Dismiss as 'probably an admin script' without reading the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Wrong from the other side. Dismissal-by-pattern would have missed the 'not legitimate' classification.

choices.label: Isolate the host immediately and bump the senior.
choices.rationale: Treating a Low as Critical based on pattern alone.

questions.explanation: The SOC's classification, not the OS-level pattern, drove the response. Standard Low workflow.

questions.prompt: "If it looks like the RMM, it's the RMM." What does the lesson say?
choices: Sometimes. Read the SOC's classification, the SOC has access to information about your RMM's normal patterns that you can confirm at the moment.

choices.label: Always, RMM sources are trusted by default.
choices.rationale: The misconception. Trusting source-by-appearance is the dismissal-by-pattern failure.

choices.label: Never, RMM sources should be treated as suspicious by default.
choices.rationale: The opposite misconception. Treating all RMM-shaped activity as suspicious is the escalation-by-pattern failure.

choices.label: Only if the customer's IT contact confirms in writing.
choices.rationale: Customer confirmation can be new signal, but it's not the test. The Recommendation is.

questions.explanation: Read the Recommendation, not the pattern.

questions.prompt: You read a benign-classified process detection. You have new signal the SOC didn't have, the user phoned to say they didn't initiate the activity. What do you do?
choices: Reply to the SOC with the new signal, don't act unilaterally. New signal routes through the analyst, not through tech-seat re-classification.

choices.label: Close the incident as benign anyway, the SOC has already classified.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The user denial IS new signal the SOC didn't have.

choices.label: Approve a remediation unilaterally on the basis of the user denial.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Unilateral remediation against the SOC's 'no action' classification is the second-guessing failure.

choices.label: Escalate to a senior immediately as a Critical.
choices.rationale: Premature. The SOC's clarifying reply is the right channel.

questions.explanation: New signal goes back to the SOC. Tech-seat re-classification isn't the answer.

questions.prompt: When a process detection lands, the tech should investigate the process themselves to be sure of the classification.
questions.explanation: Same family of mistake as second-guessing the SOC. The Recommendation is the action zone; tech-seat investigation duplicates the SOC's work with worse tooling.
